response_justification.value:
Model A
- No CQ rule violation, well structured with hypothesis -> repro table -> numbered fix-> adverserial-testing flow.

Model B
- No CQ rule violation, tightest of the three; leads with the root cause, uses compact before-after repro table, and ends on an honestly flagged known limitation.

Model C
- Fails O4 — at around 3000 characters it is the longest response, and it carries its before-after comparison in bullets rather than a scannable table, so the same info takes more reading.

---
Mo

code_quality_justification.value:
Model B is the best as it fixes the reported bug, handles more transaction forms than C, and verifies most thoroughly. It is better than C as it also handles named/qualified transactions; revising until they passed; and records the change in the CHANGELOG, where C scopes those cases out.

items.content: I’m seeing weird `sqlparse.split()` behavior with `BEGIN ...` SQL. Sometimes it keeps everything as one statement, and formatting changes (newlines/comments) make the results inconsistent. Please debug this carefully and don’t jump to the first fix. I want you to form a hypothesis, verify it with small repro cases, implement a fix, then actively try to break your own fix with edge cases before finalizing. If something fails, revise and retest until behavior is stable. Plan first, then implement, and validate each step before finalizing.
items.title: Current Prompt

trace.trace:
## Summary

### Hypothesis
`sqlparse.split()` treated `BEGIN;` as a transaction (split OK) but failed with `BEGIN\n;` because **newlines were not recognized as whitespace** when deciding whether to keep the `_seen_begin` flag.

Root cause in `statement_splitter.py`:
```python
ttype not in (T.Whitespace, T.Comment.Single, T.Comment.Multiline)
```
uses Python **tuple membership** (identity), while e

+# Tokens that may appear between BEGIN and ';' in a transaction statement

+    'COMMITTED', 'UNCOMMITTED', 'REPEATABLE', 'SERIALIZABLE', 'SNAPSHOT',
+    'DEFERRABLE', 'NOT',
+})
+

trace.trace:
## What was going wrong

**Hypothesis:** The splitter distinguishes transaction `BEGIN;` from `BEGIN…END` blocks with a `_seen_begin` flag. That flag was cleared by newlines between `BEGIN` and `;` because the code used tuple equality instead of hierarchical token-type membership.

**Verified root cause:** `\n` lexes as `Token.Text.Whitespace.Newline`. This check:

```python
ttype not in (T.Whites
trace.messages:

**Verified root cause:** `\n` lexes as `Token.Text.Whitespace.Newline`. This check:

diff.codeDiff:
diff --git a/CHANGELOG b/CHANGELOG
index cd5fc11..d87fe10 100644
--- a/CHANGELOG
+++ b/CHANGELOG
@@ -1,7 +1,14 @@
 Development Version
 -------------------
 
-Nothing yet.
+Bug Fixes
+
+* Fix ``sqlparse.split()`` treating ``BEGIN`` followed by a newline (or
+  comments) before ``;`` as an open ``BEGIN...END`` block. Newlines are
+  ``Token.Whitespace.Newline``, and the splitter incorrectly cleared its
+  transaction-BEGIN marker because it used tuple equality instead of
+  hierarchical token-type membership. Also recognize common transaction
+  forms such as ``BEGIN TRANSACTION`` / ``BEGIN WORK`` / ``BEGIN TRAN``.
 
 
 Release 0.5.4 (Nov 28, 2025)

index 3e028f8..926c1ed 100644
--- a/sqlparse/engine/s

trace.trace:
The work is complete and stable. Here's a summary of what I found and did.

## Root cause

The inconsistent `sqlparse.split()` behavior with `BEGIN` came from how the statement splitter decides whether a `BEGIN` starts a **transaction** (like `BEGIN;`, where the following `;` should split) versus a **`BEGIN...END` block** (where inner semicolons should not split).

The splitter uses a `_seen_begin
